message:
fix(setup): propagate CSPRNG failures instead of silently returning empty/panicking tokens

Before this change, `generate_token` called `getrandom::getrandom(&mut bytes)`
and threw the result away with `let _ = …`. On CSPRNG failure (locked-down
seccomp sandboxes, very old kernels with `/dev/urandom` gone, LXC without
`CAP_SYS_ADMIN`, etc.) the 32-byte buffer stayed at its zero-initialized
default, and `generate_token` would hand back the all-zeros hex string
`"0000…0000"` as a "bearer token". `generate_registration_token` already
did the safer thing — but via `.expect(...)`, which turns the same failure
into a process-wide panic in whatever async task happens to be generating
the token. Both behaviors are wrong for a security-relevant primitive:
silent zero-entropy tokens are worse than an error, and a panic tears
down unrelated in-flight work.

This change:

- Returns `Result<String, SetupError>` from `generate_token`,
  `generate_registration_token`, and `resolve_token`. The error carries
  the underlying `getrandom::Error` in a `SetupError::Other` so callers
  can log/report it.
- Introduces `fill_random_bytes(&mut [u8])` as the single choke point
  that consults the CSPRNG. Both generators go through it so a future
  switch of source (e.g. `rand_core::OsRng`) is a one-line change.
- Adds a `TEST_RANDOM_FAILURE` thread-local + `RandomFailureGuard` so
  `setup.rs` can deterministically simulate a CSPRNG failure inside the
  test harness without actually disabling the host RNG. The guard is
  RAII-scoped so concurrent tests cannot leak the failure flag.
- Propagates the new `Result` at both CLI call sites —
  `run_setup_self_heal_for_server` and `handle_setup` — by mapping into
  `CliError::Other` so the user gets an actionable "setup token
  resolution failed: …" message instead of a random panic or a silent
  zero-token outcome.

Tests added:

- `generate_token_reports_rng_failure` pins the new error surface.
- Every existing `resolve_token` / `generate_token` test is updated to
  `.expect(...)` the success case so the type-system change is covered
  end-to-end.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
